text: The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper to carefully reduce the omentum protruding into the defect of an indirect inguinal hernia on the left side. The assistant aids by pushing the hernia contents from above, facilitating the reduction process.

The surgeon has successfully reduced all hernia contents and is now preparing to perform the IPOM (Intraperitoneal Onlay Mesh) technique. The inferior border of the mesh is being sutured to the peritoneum using a personal suturing technique.

And we have a very encouraging result with the Vipro mess. So you don't have to do any dissection of the sac. You don't have to do any danger of going to the triangle of dome. Just here sigma and colon is a little bit mobilized.
The surgeon positions a 15x15 cm mesh over the hernia defect, ensuring a 6 cm margin around the healthy tissue. The mesh is centered over the defect, and the surgeon begins securing it with a tacker.

The surgeon uses an absorbable polygalactane tacker to secure the mesh around the hernia defect, avoiding areas such as the triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster. Care is taken to avoid the inferior epigastric vessel and bladder while positioning the mesh above the inguinal ligament.

The surgeon uses a supraumbilical port to position the tacker on the rectus abdominis, firing at least ten centimeters above the upper border of the pubic symphysis and on the Cooper ligament. The technique avoids firing below the medial umbilical ligament to prevent damage to the bladder area, ensuring no bleeding occurs during the procedure.

Previously people were using the fibrin glue but with the absorbent tacker and suture we have very encouraging result and we don't have any recurrence and we don't have any shiroma also so to two centimeter keeping to two centimeter all around you can fire it and care should be taken that left hand finger should oppose the abdominal wall from above so that tip of the tacker should be perpendicular while you are firing

The surgeon secures the Y-Pro-II mesh, which has inner layers made of proline and an outer weave of Y-Trail, by anchoring it around the defect. The mesh is fixed over the rectus abdominis muscle and Cooper's ligament, ensuring not to fire over the trapezoid of disaster, triangle of doom, and triangle of pain.

The surgeon uses a Dundee jamming knot to secure the lower edge of the mesh, employing continuous suturing and everting termination to prevent recurrence. The inferomedial end of the mesh is carefully fixed after pulling the peritoneum.

So as soon as you will pull the needle and pass it through the loop and pull it automatically it get locked. And this knot is locking knot. It is not like a pre-tied loop. It is a beautiful knot and this is popular and it was started first by Professor Sir Alfred Cushieri in Dundee. So that's why it is called a Dundee jamming knot. And as you already you know that

The surgeon discusses the Aberdeen termination knot, emphasizing the need to stretch the peritoneum before taking a bite to avoid the obturator vessel. Care is taken not to prick the peritoneum in this sensitive area.

The surgeon carefully lifts the peritoneum and brings it near the tip of the needle, ensuring the needle does not penetrate deeper than necessary. The lower edge of the peritoneum is secured with locking sutures to prevent any gaps.

The surgeon carefully ties and locks each knot to ensure the mesh is completely sealed inferiorly, preventing any bowel from lifting the mesh and entering the defect. The peritoneum is visible, and the surgeon avoids pricking it to prevent injury to the external iliac vein or artery.

The surgeon interlocks sutures to reinforce the mesh placement, carefully lifting the peritoneum and positioning it over the needle to avoid damaging the external iliac vein and artery. This technique minimizes the risk of pricking the iliac vessels, which could lead to serious complications.

So this is little tricky and little dangerous that's why people they are reluctant to do the eye palm hernia surgery by suturing Many surgeons have published the article of doing the eye palm hernia surgery but what they do that in the lower part they use fibrin glue and as we know that fibrin glue is expensive and it may not be possible for every patient to get the fibrin glue because the two ml fibrin glue is costing you approximately fifteen thousand rupees.
The surgeon carefully lifts the peritoneum while navigating past the triangle of doom, taking care not to prick the spermatic vessels located at the lateral boundary of this anatomical area.

The surgeon completes the aberrant termination of the suture in the laparotomy closure of the abdominal wall. The lower edge is visibly adhered to the mesh, securing the defect area. The suture is cut, ensuring no omentum or bowel can enter the defect area. The needle is removed, concluding the eye palm technique for inguinal hernia repair.
